title: Valve's Steam Machine ships June 29 for $1,049, but you probably won't be able to buy one yet
gist: Valve's Steam Machine ships June 29 starting at $1,049, with pricing and supply both squeezed by the AI-boom-driven surge in RAM and storage costs; Valve admits its original sub-$1,000 vision is no longer viable and is using a randomized reservation system due to component shortages.

insights.claim: The AI boom sent storage and RAM prices sharply higher, forcing Valve to delay the Steam Machine and price it from $1,049 — Valve admits its original sub-$1,000 vision (when sourcing began in 2023) is 'no longer viable' in 2026.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that AI-driven memory demand is raising consumer-hardware costs across the industry; a margin and pricing headwind for PC/console makers.
insights.quote: Valve admits the original vision is "no longer viable" in 2026.
insights.locator: According to Valve, it did not set out

insights.claim: Component cost inflation has pushed prices up across the sector: Sony's base PS5 is now $600 and the Pro $900, Nintendo's Switch 2 rose from $450 to $500, and Valve raised the Steam Deck by more than $200.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Broad-based gaming-hardware price increases signal eroding consumer affordability and squeezed maker margins amid the memory crunch.

insights.claim: Valve was at times unable to obtain memory and storage parts at any price, limiting Steam Machine supply and prompting a randomized one-per-household reservation lottery to combat resellers and bots.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Supply constraints, not just pricing, are limiting hardware launches; the memory shortage is severe enough to cap availability outright.
insights.quote: The company was, at times, unable to get the parts it needed to build Steam Machines at any price

insights.claim: Valve signaled Steam Machine pricing could rise in future batches as component prices continue to climb, since current prices reflect only the past six months' procurement costs.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Forward price increases are flagged as likely if memory inflation persists — a watch item for hardware demand elasticity.
insights.locator: That suggests the pricing could change
